document_text:
Pointy – A Lightweight Transformer for Point Cloud Foundati on
Models
Konrad Szafer∗ORCID: 0009-0005-6900-0484
Marek Kraft∗ORCID: 0000-0001-6483-2357
Dominik Belter∗ORCID: 0000-0003-3002-9747
Abstract
Foundation models for point cloud data have recently grown in capab ility, often leveraging
extensive representation learning from language or vision. In this w ork, we take a more con-
trolled approach by introducing a lightweight transformer-based p oint cloud architecture. In
contrast to the heavy reliance on cross-modal supervision, our m odel is trained only on 39k
point clouds - yet it outperforms several larger foundation models trained on over 200k train-
ing samples. Interestingly, our method approaches state-of-th e-art results from models that
have seen over a million point clouds, images, and text samples, demon strating the value of a
carefully curated training setup and architecture. To ensure rigo rous evaluation, we conduct a
comprehensive replication study that standardizes the training re gime and benchmarks across
multiple point cloud architectures. This uniﬁed experimental framew ork isolates the impact
of architectural choices, allowing for transparent comparisons a nd highlighting the beneﬁts of
our design and other tokenizer-free architectures. Our results show that simple backbones can
deliver competitive results to more complex or data-rich strategies1.
To appear in the proceedings of ACIVS 2025. An earlier versio n was presented at the SCI-FM workshop at
ICLR 2025.
1 Introduction
3D representation learning has grown increasingly important acros s domains such as robotics, augmented
reality, and computational design. Recent advances in model arch itectures [8, 31, 5, 18, 29], representation
learning [28, 13, 16, 30, 32], and even multimodal large language mode ls that incorporate point clouds [9, 27,
12]havepushedtheboundariesin3Dperception. However,robus t3Dfeatureextractionremainschallenging.
Additionally, current training protocols and evaluation environment s for point cloud architectures diverge
in the literature, hindering fair and transparent method compariso n. Consequently, many architectural
choicesareguided byexperimentalsetups diﬀeringin dataregimes, preprocessing,orhyperparametertuning,
limiting unbiased conclusions about any given design’s strengths.
Meanwhile, the concept of foundation models has taken hold in other domains such as natural language
processing [3, 22, 1], computer vision [11, 4], and time series [6].
For instance, in computer vision, architectural innovations such a s the Swin Transformer [14] have
propelled state-of-the-art performance; however, as noted b y Oquab et al. [17], it remains challenging to
disentangle whether these gains primarily stem from large-scale dat asets, advanced architectures, or reﬁned
training strategies. The landscape for point cloud data is also evolvin g rapidly, with numerous approaches
∗Institute of Robotics and Machine Intelligence, Poznan Uni versity of Technology
1The implementation, including code, pre-trained models, a nd training protocols, is available at
https://github.com/KonradSzafer/Pointy .
1arXiv:2603.10963v1  [cs.CV]  11 Mar 2026

now incorporating cross-modal learning of images or text, often u sing datasets of hundreds of thousands to
millions of samples. While these eﬀorts have unquestionably expanded the frontiers of 3D representation
learning,theyalsohighlighttheurgentneedforstandardized,con trolledexperimentstoisolatetheunderlying
drivers of model performance.
In this work, we propose a lightweight, transformer-based backb one for point cloud processing that
operates directly on point coordinates without requiring a separat ely trained tokenizer. Despite using just
39kshapesand asimple classiﬁcationobjective, ourmethod outper formslargerfoundationmodels trainedon
over 200k point clouds. More surprisingly, it approaches state-of -the-art results achieved by models trained
on over a million samples from point cloud, image, and text representa tions. To ensure a robust and fair
evaluation, we conduct a replication study under a uniﬁed training an d benchmarking regime. We compare
our approach with other tokenizer-free architectures while apply ing the same preprocessing steps, optimizer
settings, and hyperparameter schedules.
Our contributions are threefold:
•1.Comprehensive replication study: We propose a benchmarking system for point cloud founda-
tion models to evaluate populartransformerbackbonesunder iden ticaltraining regimesand evaluation
metrics, providing an apples-to-apples comparison of their streng ths and limitations.
•2.Lightweight yet competitive model: We present a simple transformer-based backbone that
excels without relying on massive cross-modal supervision or large d ata sets.
•3.Controlled pre-training: We show that limited data can compete with much larger data-driven
approaches, providing practical insights into scaling strategies an d design trade-oﬀs.
2 Related Work
Point Cloud Deep Learning Architectures. Early work like PointNet and PointNet++ [19, 20], laid
the foundation for deep learning on irregular 3D data by introducing permutation invariant architectures
that operate directly on unordered point sets. Building on these se minal ideas, subsequent methods have
explored graph-based models [25] and convolutional operators to better capture local and global geometric
features. More recently, transformer-based approaches [8, 31, 5, 18] have emerged as a promising direction,
exploiting self-attention to model long-range dependencies inhere nt in point clouds. In parallel, advances
in self-supervised representation learning [18, 21, 29] have furth er reﬁned these architectures and training
paradigms by enabling robust feature extraction even under limited supervision.
Foundation Models and Cross-Modal Representation Learnin g.The emergence of foundation
models in natural language processing [3, 22, 1], computer vision [11, 4], and other modalities [6] has un-
derscored the transformative impact of large-scale pre-training and uniﬁed architectures. Inspired by these
successes, recent eﬀorts in 3D representation learning have beg un to incorporate cross-modal supervision,
aligning point clouds with image and text modalities [9, 27, 12] to improv e performance.
However, the signiﬁcant data requirements and complex training pip elines of these multimodal models
raise questions about their true success drivers.
3 Pointy
We present Pointy, our lightweight transformer-based model for point cloud proces sing. The main goal is to
jointly learn the patch-level embeddings and the global represent ation in a single architecture while keeping
the core transformer block unchanged. Unlike some of the previou s works that rely on replaced attention
layersor separatetokenization schemes, our approach learns em beddings directly from points, complemented
by positional information. An overview of the proposed pipeline is give n in Fig. 1.
3.1 Point Embeddings
To encode the raw point cloud into a compact set of feature tokens , we adopt a simpliﬁed PointNet-like [19]
embedding module, scaled down to ﬁt our goal of lightweight design. I nspired by residual connections [11],
2

jectNN benchmarks . We report overall accuracy (OA), model parameters, and FLO Ps (in G),

tothe[-1,1] range, andrandomz-axis rotation. Resultsdem onstratethecompetitive performanceof
our lightweight architecture (3.0M parameters, 16.2G FLOP s) across diverse point cloud datasets.
Models marked with [T]denote transformer-based architectures, while those with [ST]incorporate
an unchanged transformer block.
Model #Points Param. (M) ↓FLOPs (G) ↓ModelNet40 ScanObjectNN
OA (%) ↑OA (%) ↑

we include them, so absolute coordinates pass with learned feature s, preserving geometric information. In
addition, we add a learnable positional embedding [4], which provides e ncoding of the spatial arrangement
of the patches.
Following common practice in vision transformers, we aggregate loca l patches of points before feeding
them into the backbone. Speciﬁcally, we use Farthest Point Sampling (FPS) for anchor points, then group
theirk-nearest neighbors (kNN) as in [31]. Each neighborhood is transfor med into a feature vector via our
embedding layer and combined with a learned positional token. This to kenizer-free strategy reduces the
complexity of handling irregular input while preserving ﬁne-grained loc al geometry. Our method can be
trained on R3×N(3D coordinates) and R6×N(3D coordinates + normals/color).
3.2 Transformer Backbone
We feed the resulting embeddings into a hierarchical transformer d esigned to handle up to 64 patch tokens
but easily adjustable through conﬁgurable merging ratios in one of it s layers. The transformer consists of
six layers of multi-head self-attention, with patch merging operatio ns to progressively reduce the spatial
footprint of the token set. Each layer uses a relatively low embeddin g dimension to attentional heads ratio
(approx. 3:1), introducing an inductive bias that aligns with the 3D co ordinate structure of point clouds
and promotes spatially coherent attention. By default, we use the GeLU activation function and standard
layer normalization throughout. All network weights are initialized us ing the Kaiming method [10].
We introduce two Pointy size variants for diﬀerent resource const raints and pre-training requirements.
The smaller model, with a 192-dimensional embedding, includes approx imately 3M parameters, while the
larger version, with 20M parameters, scales the embedding dimensio n to 510. Both variants deliver compet-
itive performance across a variety of classiﬁcation tasks and reso lutions (Tab. 1 and 2). In addition, our
models achieve very competitive performance on zero-shot tasks 3 when pre-trained on 39k point clouds,
compared to the state-of-the-art foundation models trained on signiﬁcantly larger datasets.
3

Figure 1: Architecture Pointy – transformer backbone for po int cloud processing. The model takes
raw point cloud data as input, applies patch partitioning us ing Farthest Point Sampling (FPS),
and k-Nearest Neighbors (kNN). Raw point features are prese rved through residual connections
alongside learned patch embeddings in the embedding layer b ased on PointNet. The architecture
consists of token merging operations between adjacent toke ns after each transformer block. This
hierarchical design enables local and global feature learn ing through progressive patch merging.
The ﬁnal output produces P ×D dimensional representations, where P is the number of patc hes and
D is the embedding dimension.
Table 2: Zero-shot Evaluation of Transformer-based Models Pre-trained on the
Objaverse-LVIS Subset. All models are pre-trained on an 85/15 split of LVIS data (85% train-
ing, 15% testing) drawn from a curated 39k-sample subset spa nning 1,156 LVIS-annotated object
categories. Training is performed for 30 epochs under ident ical conditions—batch size 16, 2k points
per sample normalized to the [-1,1] range, and random rotati ons about the z-axis. The checkpoints
obtained after this training are subsequently used for zero -shot evaluation, ensuring a consistent
and fair comparison of transferability.

4 Experimental Setup
We design a uniﬁed training environment to ensure fair comparisons a cross point cloud architectures. Unless
otherwise speciﬁed, all methods use the same data resolution, nor malization, augmentation strategies, loss
function, and optimizer conﬁguration. Below, we summarize implemen tation details used in our study.
4.1 Datasets and Tasks
ModelNet40 [26]. The dataset comprises 12,308 CAD models (40 object classes) with a predeﬁned
standard training/test split. We sample 2,048 points from each shap e and train each method for 100 epochs
4

Table 3: Zero-shot 3D Classiﬁcation on ModelNet40 and ScanObjectNN. For this eval-
uation, we discard the classiﬁcation head and directly extr act the ﬁnal-layer features from the
30th epoch checkpoint of transformer models pre-trained on the Objaverse-LVIS split (39k point
clouds). We report Top-1, Top-3, and Top-5 accuracies on bot h ModelNet40 and ScanObjectNN,
comparing our reproduced transformer baselines (e.g., PCT , two distinct PointTransformer mod-
els, and PointMAE) along with our own small and base variants against third-party foundation
models. Despite the modest dataset size relative to other la rge-scale pre-trained models, nearly all
transformer-based architectures score impressively well . Our models achieve the best performance
among them, with the PCT closely following, while outperfor ming several methods pre-trained on
up to 200k point clouds. Only two foundation models, trained on over one million point clouds and
augmented by training with representations from additiona l modalities, record higher scores. Blue
shading indicates transformer-based backbones replicate d under our experimental setting, while
yellow shading denotes reported values for foundation mode ls from the literature.

using identical hyperparameters and preprocessing. As is common practice, the metric reported is overall
classiﬁcation accuracy on the test set.
ScanObjectNN [23]. Contains 2,890 real-world 3D scans with 15 object categories, intro ducing sub-
stantially higher complexity due to noise, clutter, and occlusions. As with ModelNet40, we sample 2,048
points per scan and train for 100 epochs with the same conﬁguratio n. We report the best test accuracy
achieved by each method.

5

 (b) ScanObjectNN

Figure 2: Training dynamics of diﬀerent transformer-based models on ModelNet40,
ScanObjectNN, and Objaverse-LVIS datasets. The plots show overall classiﬁcation accuracy
(%) versus training epochs for our proposed small model comp ared to existing approaches: PCT
[8], PointMAE [18], and two variants of PointTransformer [3 1] and [5]. Our method demonstrates
faster convergence across all datasets while achieving com petitive or superior ﬁnal accuracy. The
inset plots for (a) and (b) show detailed performance in late r epochs. Notable is the consistent
underperformance of PointTransformer [5] relative to othe r methods across all datasets.
4.2 Baselines
We evaluate a diverse set of architectures, spanning both classica l point cloud networks and transformer-
based models. All implementations are sourced from oﬃcial reposito ries or widely validated reproductions,
preserving their original architectural designs and hyperparame ters (e.g., hidden dimensions, number of
layers, dropout ratios).
4.3 Implementation Details
We maintain consistent experimental conditions across all evaluatio ns: each shape is preprocessed by uni-
formly sampling 2,048 points and normalized to the [-1,1] range, with ra ndom z-axis rotation as the only
augmentation and no voting-based inference. The training utilizes t he AdamW optimizer with a ﬁxed learn-
ing rate of 1 ×10−4without scheduling, using a batch size of 16 and standard cross-en tropy loss. Models
are trained for up to 100 epochs on ModelNet40 and ScanObjectNN , and 30 epochs on Objaverse-LVIS,
reporting the best-observed test accuracy.
4.4 Results
4.4.1 Classiﬁcation
Tab. 1 shows the accuracy on ModelNet40 and ScanObjectNN using 2,048-point inputs for all architectures
compared. Classicaldeep learningmethods achievethe strongest performance, with PointNetand PointMLP
reaching91.6%onModelNet40, whereasPointNet++achieves81.7%o nScanObjectNN.Amongtransformer-
based architectures, our method leads with 90.6% on ModelNet40 an d 80.0% on ScanObjectNN (using our
small variant), surpassing other transformer approaches, and converging visibly faster.
Our controlled training setup, without learning rate scheduling and h yperparameter tuning, may partic-
ularly impact transformer-based models, which typically beneﬁt fro m longer training schedules and careful
optimization strategies. This observation suggests that the supe rior performance of classical architectures in
our experiments might partially originate from their robustness to s impliﬁed training protocols.
6

Figure 3: Classiﬁcation accuracy on ModelNet40
as a function of input point cloud size. Models were
trained for 30 epochs under identical conditions, with re-
sults showing the peak accuracy achieved. While PCT
demonstrates superior performance in the 256-1024 point
range, our architecture achieves competitive results and
attains 89.3% for 2048 points.
4.4.2 Pre-training
Next, we evaluate only transformer-based architectures on the Objaverse-LVIS subset, with results summa-
rized in Table 2. Despite the modest size of this curated dataset, ou r ”small” model achieves an accuracy
of 36.4%, and closely matches PCT [8] (36.3%) in a medium-sized variant . As before, we observe that our
approach typically converges faster than other transformer ba selines.
4.4.3 Zero-shot Evaluation
Table 3 presents our zero-shot transfer evaluation, where we us e the 30th epoch checkpoint from Objaverse-
LVIS as a foundational model, tested directly on ModelNet40 and Sc anObjectNN without ﬁne-tuning. For
this, we discard the classiﬁcation head, extract ﬁnal-layer featur es from transformer models pre-trained on
the Objaverse-LVIS split (39k point clouds), and classify test sam ples by cosine similarity to class proto-
types derived from the target dataset’s training set means. We re port Top-1, Top-3, and Top-5 accuracies,
benchmarking our reproduced transformers (e.g., PCT, PointTra nsformer variants, PointMAE, and our
Small/Base models) against literature-reported foundation models . Strikingly, our method—trained solely
on 39k shapes—outperforms all prior models pre-trained on Shape Net’s 200k point clouds, even those with
additional modalities or self-supervised objectives. Notably, Uni3D leverages a vast dataset of nearly one
million 3D shapes, 10 million images, and 70 million texts, with shapes rende red from 10 viewpoints, yet our
lightweight approach narrows the gap to state-of-the-art perf ormance. Furthermore, the end-to-end training
employed by our model, a characteristic shared with top-performin g foundation models like Uni3D, may be a
key contributor to these strong generalization capabilities. We also suspect this strong performance, despite
the smaller dataset size, is partly attributable to the high quality and clean labeling of our pre-training data.
4.4.4 Ablation Studies
As shown in Figure 3, our ﬁrst ablation study examines how classiﬁcat ion performance scales with diﬀerent
point cloud resolutions on ModelNet40. While PCT excels in the 256–102 4 point range, our architecture
remains competitive and achieves a peak accuracy of 89.3% at 2048 p oints, slightly surpassing previous
works. These ﬁndings highlight that model eﬀectiveness can vary c onsiderably with resolution, reﬂecting
distinct scaling behaviors across architectures.
Our ablation (Table 4) shows simple token embedding summation outpe rforms a linear merging layer in
the hierarchical transformer, suggesting additive merging bette r preserves geometric relationships without
unnecessary parameters. Notably, the non-hierarchical trans former also achieves competitive results, likely
due to the advantages of the chosen patching scheme and the rob ust positional embeddings that eﬀectively
guide the attention mechanism. Although we observed higher accur acy at 30 epochs with 32 and 128
patches, in practice, we chose 64 patches to balance computation al eﬃciency and representational capacity.
Furthermore, increasing embedding dimension and attention heads consistently improves performance.
7

ﬁguration parameters includingembeddingdimension, numb erof attention heads, patch count, and

training epochs (training setup detailed in Section 4.3). A ll experiments are conducted with 2048
input points.

5 Conclusion
In this paper, we introduced a lightweight, transformer-based ba ckbone for learning point cloud representa-
tions and conducted a comprehensive replication study under a uniﬁ ed training and benchmarking regime.
Our experimental results demonstrate that even with only 39k pre -training samples, the proposed archi-
tecture achieves competitive and often superior performance co mpared to much larger foundation models
trained on over 200k point clouds. Moreover, our model approach es the performance of state-of-the-art
methods that leverage over one million point clouds, images, and text samples. These results underscore
the importance of carefully selecting the training setup, architect ure, end-to-end optimization, and data
curation, rather than relying solely on extensive cross-modal sup ervision or massive datasets. Finally, our
results provide a transparent comparison of architectural choic es and highlight the promise of simple yet
powerful backbones. We hope that our open-source code, train ing protocols, and pre-trained weights will
inspire broader engagement with controlled experimentation in 3D re presentation learning and ultimately
promote reproducible and eﬃcient approaches to foundation mode ls in this rapidly evolving domain.
While the zero-shot results on classiﬁcation tasks are encouraging , they also highlight an important
limitation: ourmodel(andallotherreproductions)hasbeentraine dprimarilyunderaclassiﬁcationobjective.
This may limit the model’s ability to fully exploit the 3D geometry for ﬁner- grained or denser prediction
tasks. Furthermore, pre-training primarily on a curated, less nois y dataset might necessitate additional
ﬁne-tuning for optimal generalization to more challenging real-world scanner data.
Therefore, our future research should investigate other settin gs, in particular semantic and instance seg-
mentation in real 3D scenes, training on largerdatasets, and verif ying pre-training strategiesand multimodal
alignments (e.g., with images or text) in a similarly controlled experiment al framework.

structure.title: Introduction
structure.summary: The partial document discusses the introduction of a lightweight transformer-based point cloud architecture called Pointy. The main points covered include the development of a simple yet competitive model that operates directly on point coordinates without requiring a separately trained tokenizer, outperforming larger foundation models trained on over 200k point clouds. The document also highlights the need for standardized and controlled experiments to isolate the drivers of model performance, and proposes a benchmarking system for point cloud foundation models. Additionally, it explores the concept of foundation models, cross-modal representation learning, and related work in point cloud deep learning architectures. The authors propose a comprehensive replication study, a lightweight yet competitive model, and controlled pre-training as their key contributions.

structure.title: Related Work
structure.summary: The partial document discusses the development of a lightweight, transformer-based backbone for point cloud processing, called Pointy. The main points covered include the need for standardized experiments to evaluate the performance of 3D representation learning models, the proposal of a benchmarking system for point cloud foundation models, and the presentation of Pointy as a competitive model that excels without relying on massive cross-modal supervision or large datasets. The document also highlights the importance of controlled pre-training and the potential of limited data to compete with larger data-driven approaches. Additionally, it provides an overview of related work in point cloud deep learning architectures, foundation models, and cross-modal representation learning, as well as a detailed description of the Pointy model architecture, including its point embeddings and transformer backbone. The results of experiments comparing Pointy with other models are also presented, demonstrating its competitive performance across diverse point cloud datasets.

structure.title: Pointy
structure.summary: The partial document discusses the comparison of classification performance across ModelNet40 and ScanObjectNN benchmarks for various point cloud models, including the authors' lightweight architecture. The results demonstrate the competitive performance of the proposed model, which has 3.0M parameters and 16.2G FLOPs, across diverse point cloud datasets. The document also describes the architecture of the model, including the use of a hierarchical transformer backbone, patch embedding, and positional encoding. The model is designed to handle irregular input and preserve fine-grained local geometry, and it can be trained on 3D coordinates and normals/color. The authors also introduce two model variants with different resource constraints and pre-training requirements, which deliver competitive performance across various classification tasks and resolutions.

nodes.title: Point Embeddings
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the comparison of classification performance across different point cloud datasets, including ModelNet40 and ScanObjectNN benchmarks. It highlights the competitive performance of a lightweight architecture with 3.0M parameters and 16.2G FLOPs. The document also describes the design of a transformer-based model, called Pointy, which uses a hierarchical transformer backbone to handle point cloud data. The model's architecture, training setup, and experimental results are presented, including zero-shot evaluation on the Objaverse-LVIS subset. The main points covered include the comparison of different models, the design of the Pointy architecture, and the experimental setup and results.

structure.title: Experimental Setup
structure.summary: The partial document discusses the architecture and evaluation of transformer-based models for point cloud processing. The main points covered include the description of the Architecture Pointy model, which uses a transformer backbone with patch partitioning and residual connections to preserve raw point features. The document also presents the results of zero-shot evaluation of various transformer-based models pre-trained on the Objaverse-LVIS subset, comparing their performance in terms of parameters, FLOPs, and object recognition accuracy. Additionally, the document outlines the experimental setup, including the unified training environment, datasets, and tasks used to evaluate the models, such as ModelNet40.

nodes.title: Datasets and Tasks
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the architecture and evaluation of transformer-based models for point cloud processing. The main points covered include the description of the Architecture Pointy model, which uses a transformer backbone for point cloud processing, and the evaluation of various transformer-based models on zero-shot 3D classification tasks using datasets such as ModelNet40, ScanObjectNN, and Objaverse-LVIS. The document also presents the results of experiments comparing the performance of different models, including the authors' own models, on these tasks. The results show that the authors' models achieve the best performance among the transformer-based architectures, outperforming several methods pre-trained on larger datasets. The document also discusses the experimental setup, including the datasets, tasks, and implementation details used in the study.

nodes.title: Baselines
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the results of zero-shot 3D classification on ModelNet40 and ScanObjectNN datasets using various transformer-based models. The models are pre-trained on the Objaverse-LVIS split, which contains 39,000 point clouds, and their performance is compared to other foundation models trained on larger datasets. The results show that the transformer-based models achieve impressive performance, with the best results obtained by the authors' own small and base variants. The document also provides a table summarizing the top-1, top-3, and top-5 accuracies of the different models on both datasets, highlighting the competitive performance of the transformer-based architectures.

nodes.title: Implementation Details
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the comparison of classification performance across different models, including PointNet, PointNet++, DGCNN, and transformer-based architectures, on various point cloud datasets such as ModelNet40 and ScanObjectNN. The document highlights the competitive performance of a lightweight architecture, with 3.0M parameters and 16.2G FLOPs, across diverse point cloud datasets. It also introduces a hierarchical transformer backbone designed to handle point cloud data, which demonstrates faster convergence and competitive or superior final accuracy compared to existing approaches. The document provides tables and figures to support the comparison of different models and their performance on various datasets, including zero-shot evaluation and training dynamics. Overall, the document focuses on the evaluation and comparison of different point cloud classification models, with an emphasis on transformer-based architectures and their performance on various datasets.

nodes.title: Results
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the performance of different transformer-based models on various datasets, including ModelNet40, ScanObjectNN, and Objaverse-LVIS. The main points covered include the training dynamics and classification accuracy of these models, with a focus on the proposed small model and its comparison to existing approaches such as PCT, PointMAE, and PointTransformer. The document also describes the experimental setup, including the preprocessing of point cloud data, training parameters, and evaluation metrics. The results show that the proposed small model achieves competitive or superior accuracy on the tested datasets, while also demonstrating faster convergence. Additionally, the document highlights the performance of classical deep learning methods, such as PointNet and PointMLP, and discusses the potential impact of training protocols on the performance of transformer-based models.

nodes.title: Classification
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the performance of different transformer-based models on various datasets, including ModelNet40, ScanObjectNN, and Objaverse-LVIS. The main points covered include the training dynamics and classification accuracy of these models, with a focus on the proposed small model and its comparison to existing approaches such as PCT, PointMAE, and PointTransformer. The document also describes the experimental setup, including the preprocessing of shapes, augmentation, and training protocols, and presents the results of the classification experiments, highlighting the performance of classical deep learning methods and transformer-based architectures.

nodes.title: Pre-training
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the introduction of a lightweight transformer-based architecture called Pointy for point cloud processing. The model is trained on a relatively small dataset of 39k point clouds and achieves competitive results with larger foundation models trained on over 200k point clouds. The document highlights the importance of standardized training protocols and evaluation environments for point cloud architectures, and proposes a benchmarking system for fair comparisons. The authors also conduct a replication study to evaluate the performance of their model and other tokenizer-free architectures under identical training regimes and evaluation metrics. The results demonstrate the effectiveness of the Pointy architecture and provide insights into the design trade-offs and scaling strategies for point cloud processing. The document also discusses related work on point cloud deep learning architectures, foundation models, and cross-modal representation learning, and presents a comprehensive comparison of the proposed model with other state-of-the-art models on various benchmarks.

nodes.title: Zero-shot Evaluation
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the architecture and performance of a transformer-based model for point cloud processing. The model, called Architecture Pointy, uses a hierarchical design with patch partitioning, k-Nearest Neighbors, and residual connections to preserve raw point features. The document presents results from zero-shot evaluation on the ModelNet40 and ScanObjectNN datasets, where the model achieves competitive or superior performance compared to other transformer-based models. The model is also evaluated on the Objaverse-LVIS subset, where it achieves an accuracy of 36.4%. The document also presents ablation studies, including the effect of point cloud resolution on classification performance and the comparison of different token embedding merging methods. Overall, the document highlights the effectiveness of the proposed model for point cloud processing and its potential for generalization to other datasets.

nodes.title: Ablation Studies
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the performance of a lightweight, transformer-based architecture for learning point cloud representations. The main points covered include the model's competitive performance on the ModelNet40 dataset, its ability to achieve high accuracy with a relatively small pre-training dataset, and its strong generalization capabilities in zero-shot transfer evaluation. The document also presents ablation studies that examine the impact of different architectural components and model configuration parameters on the model's performance. Additionally, it highlights the importance of careful selection of the training setup, architecture, end-to-end optimization, and data curation in achieving superior performance. The document concludes by discussing the limitations of the model and suggesting future research directions, including investigating other settings, training on larger datasets, and verifying pre-training strategies and multimodal alignments.

structure.title: Conclusion
structure.summary: The partial document discusses the evaluation of a lightweight, transformer-based backbone for learning point cloud representations. It presents the results of an ablation study, examining the impact of various architectural components and model configuration parameters on the model's performance. The study assesses the effects of hierarchical transformer modules, token merging strategies, positional embeddings, activation functions, embedding dimension, number of attention heads, patch count, and points per patch on the model's test accuracy. The document also highlights the importance of careful selection of training setup, architecture, and data curation, and discusses the limitations and potential future research directions, including investigating other settings, such as semantic and instance segmentation, and verifying pre-training strategies and multimodal alignments.

structure.title: Acknowledgments
structure.summary: The partial document discusses the evaluation of a lightweight, transformer-based backbone for learning point cloud representations. It presents an ablation study of key architectural components, including hierarchical transformer modules, token merging strategies, positional embeddings, and activation functions. The study reports the best test accuracy on the ModelNet40 dataset over 30 training epochs. The document also provides a conclusion, highlighting the importance of carefully selecting the training setup, architecture, and data curation, rather than relying solely on extensive cross-modal supervision or massive datasets. Additionally, it discusses future research directions, including investigating other settings, such as semantic and instance segmentation in real 3D scenes, and verifying pre-training strategies and multimodal alignments. The document also includes acknowledgments, references, and a list of cited works. Overall, the main points covered in the partial document are the evaluation of a transformer-based backbone for point cloud representations, the importance of careful training setup and architecture selection, and future research directions in 3D representation learning.

structure.title: References
structure.summary: The partial document appears to be a list of references related to computer vision, machine learning, and natural language processing, specifically focusing on 3D point cloud understanding, language models, and transformer architectures. The references cover a wide range of topics, including point cloud classification, segmentation, generation, and editing, as well as language models and their applications in 3D understanding. The document also mentions various techniques, such as attention mechanisms, contrastive learning, and masked autoencoders, which are used to improve the performance of models in these areas. Overall, the main points covered in the partial document are related to the development and application of deep learning models for 3D point cloud understanding and language models, with a focus on transformer architectures and multimodal learning.
